title: Red Wine on Cotton
stainChemistry: Red wine is a tannin stain — anthocyanin polyphenols give it the color, and those anthocyanins bond to cellulose through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. Cotton is pure cellulose: chemically durable, tolerates enzymes, acidic tannin spotters, and oxygen-side oxidizers (3% H₂O₂). The anthocyanin bond is resistant to water alone but vulnerable to oxidation and to acid-side disruption. Fresh stains move under hot water; aged stains need the oxidizer or acid-side tannin lane.
whyThisWorks: Fresh wine: a cold flush carries soluble pigment out before it can set. The canonical pro sequence is NSD to emulsify → acid-side tannin spotter to break hydrogen bonds → H₂O₂ to oxidize the residual chromophore → NSD flush to remove all chemistry before heat. White cotton's indifference to heat and oxidizers lets the whole sequence run aggressively. Aged stains skip the hot-water pour-through because heat sets older tannin; they go straight to the acid-side + oxidizer loop.
defaultAssumption: Assume fresh unless the user says otherwise — fresh wine on cotton is one of the easiest tannin wins. Aged (overnight+) jumps a tier in difficulty but stays in home/pro scope; only heat-set wine (already washed hot or ironed) trends toward escalation.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water through the BACK of the fabric. Blot hard from underneath. Cotton absorbs fast, so hydraulic pressure pushes soluble pigment out the way it came in. Cold only — hot water on aged wine sets the tannin. On fresh wine (<30 minutes), 70–80% of the stain lifts here.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) in cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD diluted in cold water to the stain. Sponge gently — NSD suspends remaining anthocyanin pigment so the next steps can act on what's bound, not what's loose. Blot with a clean white cotton towel. You should see noticeable lightening before moving on.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Kettle of hot (not boiling) water, catch basin or towel underneath
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only for stains under 30 minutes old. Stretch the fabric taut over a bowl, pour hot — not boiling — water through from the back. Heat breaks hydrogen bonds on fresh tannin. Skip entirely on aged stains: heat sets older tannin permanently. This step is a fresh-wine express lane; aged wine goes straight to step 4.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: If any orange-brown shadow remains, apply a professional acid-side tannin spotter by dropper. Acid-side chemistry disrupts the anthocyanin-cellulose hydrogen bond. Dwell 2–5 minutes, blot with a clean cotton towel. Flip the towel frequently. Repeat once if the shadow persists before moving to the oxidizer step.

For residual chromophore after the acid spotter pass, apply 3% hydrogen peroxide drop-by-drop. Peroxide oxidizes the conjugated anthocyanin system to colorless products. Dwell 3–5 minutes, blot, and repeat once if any shadow survives the first pass. Escalate if two oxidizer passes don't clear it. Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between with NSD.

Flush thoroughly with NSD from the back. Residual oxidizer or acid-side spotter in the fiber will discolor under dryer heat. Rinse until the water runs clear. Launder hot per the care label — cotton is heat-durable and hot wash clears any final trace.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — fresh wine often clears 70%+ right here.
2. NSD in cold water. Sponge, dwell 2–3 min, blot.
3. FRESH ONLY (<30 min): hot pour-through from the back. Skip on aged stains.
4. Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — dropper, dwell 2–5 min, blot. Repeat once.
5. 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual chromophore. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Repeat once if shadow persists. Escalate if two passes don't clear it.
6. NSD flush until clean. Hot wash per care label. Never layer oxidizers.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout spotting; hot wash (60°C+) on whites after chemistry is fully flushed
warnings: Never use hot water on aged wine — sets tannin permanently
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, NSD-flushed between
Flush all chemistry before heat — residual oxidizer yellows under dryer

homeSolutions.instruction: The clock matters — fresh red wine on cotton is one of the easiest stains to fully erase; aged wine gets harder every hour. Cold water only while the stain is still visible. Hot water on aged wine permanently sets the tannin into the cotton fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot from the Back of the Fabric
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 30–60 seconds. The wine pushes out the way it came in. Then press a clean white towel onto the stain from underneath — don't rub. Most fresh wine lifts in this step alone.

If the spill is under 30 minutes old AND the care label doesn't forbid hot water: stretch the fabric taut over a bowl, place a clean towel underneath, and pour hot (not boiling) water through from the back. You'll see the stain lighten dramatically. Skip this step if the wine's been sitting longer than 30 minutes — heat sets older stains.

Mix 1 teaspoon Dawn dish soap, 2 tablespoons white vinegar, and 1 cup cold water in a bowl. Dab onto the stain with a clean white cloth, let sit 3 minutes, blot from center outward. The soap lifts pigment the water didn't; the vinegar (a mild acid) helps break the remaining tannin bond. Repeat once if color is still visible.
Flush with cold water from the reverse side until your blotting towel comes away clean. No soap or vinegar residue should remain before the next step — oxidizers and residues don't mix well.

homeSolutions.agent: OxiClean Soak for Any Lingering Color
homeSolutions.instruction: If you can still see a pink or brown shadow: mix one scoop of OxiClean in 1 cup cool water, fully dissolved. Soak the stained area for 15–30 minutes. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide — layering oxidizers produces toxic fumes. One oxidizer, then flush, then the next if needed.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows — cotton handles heat. Check the stain BEFORE you put it in the dryer. Any residual tannin sets permanently under dryer heat. If you can still see the stain, repeat the soap/vinegar/OxiClean pass before drying.

Escalate if: the wine's been through a hot wash and a dryer, the garment is 100% dark/dyed cotton you can't bleach, or the stain survived two full home rounds. Tell them: "Red wine on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with dish soap + vinegar + OxiClean."

steps: 1. Move fast — cold water only while the stain is visible.
2. Flush cold from the BACK of the fabric, blot with a clean white towel.
3. Fresh (<30 min) only: hot-water pour-through from the back.
4. Dish soap + white vinegar + cold water solution — dab, dwell, blot.
5. Flush cold from the back until towel runs clean.
6. OxiClean soak 15–30 minutes if shadow remains. Never layer with bleach or peroxide.
7. Hot machine wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE drying — heat sets residual tannin.
8. Escalate if heat-set, two rounds failed, or dark cotton that can't bleach.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the wine has already been through a hot wash + dryer cycle, if the cotton is dark dyed, or if two home rounds haven't cleared it. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: Hot water is a fresh-stain tool only — sets older tannin permanently
Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide
Always inspect under good light BEFORE running through a dryer

neverDo: Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never use hot water on aged wine — sets tannin permanently
Never dry with residual chemistry in the fiber — discolors under heat
Chlorine bleach on whites only; never on dyed cotton
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, acid-side tannin spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot water
Dyed cotton: test acid-side spotters on inside seam first
Heat-safe overall — cotton is the most chemistry-tolerant common fiber

materialWarnings: Hot water only for fresh wine (<30 min) — sets aged tannin
One oxidizer at a time — flush with NSD between passes
Flush all chemistry before heat — residual oxidizer yellows under dryer

escalation.when: Wine heat-set by hot wash or dryer, dark/dyed cotton that can't take bleach, or two full home rounds without clearing it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with [dish soap + vinegar / OxiClean]. [Washed hot / not yet laundered]."

contextQuestions: How long ago did the spill happen?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer since?
Any solvent or bleach tried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh + white cotton = easy full win. Aged + dark cotton = skip bleach, oxidizer + acid-side only. Heat-set = pro territory.
